item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yet another case of PL being inept and downright useless as a company. Dexter's Laboratory material, really. I wouldn't be lying if I told you that they are the worst GoI, in my opinion.

But the skip itself is funny and interesting so I'll grant you a begrudging upvote.

item.title: Re: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded: Wow, this is very cool. Thanks.

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
For such a short and simple article, I found the conceit of "Whoopsie" to be pretty funny. Have a sound file:

https://youtu.be/gZNu30rT56A

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let's improve this ant… by adding a squirrel to it!
Genius!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yup, they're homographs, not homophones/homonyms.

+ boring grammar stuff
- boring grammar stuff

Technically, they're not even always that. In russian, there are 6 grammatical cases, and words changes depending on what case they're in and whether they're plural or not. So, for these two words, all possible cases would look like this:

Case Squirrel Squirrels Protein Proteins
Nominativus белка белки белок белки
Genitivus белки белок белка белков
Dativus белке белкам белку белкам
Accusativus белку белок белок белки
Ablativus белкой белками белком белками
Praepositionalis белке белках белке белках

So they only end up being homographs about half of the time, or if you're confused about which case they're in.

If that strains your suspension of disbelief about the possibility of such a mistranslation, I refer back to the fact that this skip is based on a real-life incident in which that exact mistranslation had occurred.

Anyway, since any communications between PL and the creators of the squirrels were presumably done in a written form, the fact that they're not homophones doesn't really change much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Btw, in russian language these two words have accents on different letters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh! I would LOVE to know about this real-life incident, anqxyr!

(and I also must find out how chat works. So far, every time I try to join, I stop at the explanations and go 'What the fuck is this about??? Why does it have to be so complicated???' My main problem being that I don't like installing new software on my puter. Last time I tried I got invaded by malware, viruses and ten billion useless ads…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The word confusion thing is kind of funny, but this article requires me to believe that a bioengineering firm is going to engage in what is presumably a yearslong project with intensive resource needs without a detailed request from the contracting entity (any proposal longer than a cocktail napkin would have described what they wanted in enough specificity that they would get that they're not actually asking for squirrels). Or that the firm never once stopped to ask if what they wanted was really a bunch of crazy squirrel monsters.

This relies too much on the existence of Prometheus Labs and the attendant lolwacky incompetence meme that lies at its heart, and this Portal-centric view of science that seems to have leaked out into the popular consciousness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In-universe, there would many of them, each doing their own little thing. The five listed would most certainly not be enough to accomplish what they were intended to do (even if they can't do it now anyway).

Out-of-universe, I don't like lists like this to be twenty items longs, because it would delay the punchline too much. Besides, word limit.

Hence, the compromise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why not? There doesn't seem to be a reason to leave them out. If they're boring or unimportant, why not leave them out entirely?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's pretty much how this went in my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my mind, it's not the Prometheus who did the mistranslating, but the company they hired. So PL still fucked up by hiring some idiots, but not in the actual translation. The Foundation doesn't know this though, they're just hypothesizing.

I mentioned this in chat before posting, this is actually based on real life incident that happened. Because of this, I find it more believable that it's the Russian party that fucked up the translation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They are basically way cruder than they were expected to be. The fusing and other stuff was supposed to be happening on the cellular level, but their paws are too big to do that now, and when they attempt to scale their orders to their own size, the results just collapse under their own weight.

So they could attach themselves to you and morph into something that would benefit you somehow, e.g. improve toxin filtration or some such. But they can't manufacture normal or above-normal organs as they were intended to. Also you have a squirrel hanging from you that you now can't remove. Not the best arrangement.

PL were very much not happy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fuse with existing non-anomalous organisms and modify themselves to improve the host.

While SCP-2797 were intended to be used in cellular surgery and artificial organ manufacture, they are unable to perform these activities due to their scale and physical limitations.

These seem to be contradictory. Doesn't fusing with and improving the host include artificial organ manufacture? Is there there anything stopping the -F type from becoming a functioning liver, for example?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The mechanism under which the squirrels function strikes me as intensely abiological and aphysical and basically generally pretty impossible.

Which is probably not really a barrier to me liking this, actually.

I guess I'm still fairly pun-phobic, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think of it as more Aperture Science eagerness. Bright guys, absolutely no common sense.

"They want us to make… cellular regenerating squirrels? Allright! Dukov, pack your bags, we are going squirrel hunting!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted because even a clever linguistic pun is still a pun and because confusing the words for squirrels and proteins doesn't logically let you make magic squirrels. I prefer my mad science to be more science than magic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh. Was going to research this A-B-E-F-K… Thought it was some sort of official classification for proteins!

Okay. This is simply too funny to downvote for technical reasons, i.e. skimpy special containment procedures.

However, it is indeed a pity that the joke has to be spelled out. Could you, for instance, caption your image in Russian with translation 'protein' in English, and have a footnote (or a strikethrough) correcting the translation? I don't know if it would work in-universe. Just an idea. And I think it would actually lower your word count…!
No, wait, Prometheus made the mistake, not the Foundation. Oh never mind!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still doesn't work for me. I would see normal containment procedures as something like, "Put them in a standard containment terrarium and feed them squirrel food." Figuring out how to jump the gap between that and "deny them all biological material, presumably including food, trees, and each other" is waaaay non-trivial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why does it go A-B-E-F-K?

Because there are more variants in between that aren't mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why does it go A-B-E-F-K?

So I love "it is believed that SCP-2797 were created due to a mistranslation." but overall, this is just a little too weird without any wow. I mean, the end is cute and funny, but it's also too explainy, as Scantron said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, Prometheus Labs.
So much !!!SCIENCE!!!.
So little competent management.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, this is pretty cute. I like the biological facts as well. +1

item.title: Note to Faminepulse, if he ever revisits
item.content:encoded:
This still doesn't clear you from doing Fifthist Russian (or were they Mongolian?) squirrels.

As for the article itself, it's indeed silly. Not much else to say about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The joke kinda loses its impact on non-Russian speakers like myself, where it has to be spelled out to make sense, but despite that I still find it too amusing to not upvote. Protein squirrels, assemble!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pros:

  • I like the fact they make anomalous squirrels instead of proteins due to mistranslation. It's quite wacky of an idea.
  • This makes me wonder what is their intended purpose though…

Cons:

  • It is implied they are created, although that is not really addressed in here.

Verdict: Upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If that helps, I'm not sure I'd add anything more to the containment even if this weren't for the contest. I do usually write rather skimpy containment procedures, because they usually aren't really relevant to the core of my articles, and are just there because the format demands it, so I try to get past them as soon as possible. Also, sticking things in cells/pits/whatever is not special containment procedures, in my opinion. It's just normal containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did *like this one, particularly after the homophone was explained.

I can't quite upvote this one, however, and sadly it's a wordcount issue. I just think the containment procedures are way too skimpy. I mean, yes, don't let them come into contact with biological matter, but where is the Foundation supposed to put ~800 squirrels that can't come into contact with biological matter (including food, one would assume)? If you take -Ks description, you can't even put them with eachother! Do you cram them in individual lockers? Are they all in one big pit? What?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2797 were created by a Prometheus Labs subcontractor located in Nizhnevartovsk, Russia. Due to the displayed anomalous properties, and the fact that "proteins" and "squirrels" are homographs in the Russian language, it is believed that SCP-2797 were created due to a mistranslation.

I want to like the article, but this is a really bad case of being too explanatory… I mean, there really isn't a way you could have gotten this across to English-speakers otherwise, but I think that's a defect in the concept, rather than something I can simply excuse. No vote.

Maybe get in contact with one of the Russians and get them to do a write-up of it in Russian, see how it goes on that wiki.

EDIT: Also, this could be a lot better if it were longer and more fleshed-out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, this is very silly.

Thanks to everyone who gave me their feedback.

Image is a CC-BY via Flickr

This should be 236 words by my count.
